title: anthropic vs. openai
summary: Matthew Berman published a video comparing Anthropic and OpenAI, two major AI companies. The video is accompanied by promotional materials including an eBook on OpenClaw use cases and newsletter signup.
writeup: A live interview with Ara Khachaturyan, economist at Ramp, unpacking how Ramp's dataset of 50,000 businesses and $100 billion in annual spend gives rare ground-level visibility into AI adoption at the enterprise level. The conversation focuses on what Ramp's receipt and invoice data reveals about OpenAI versus Anthropic revenue growth, which models enterprises are actually purchasing, and how that spending is shifting toward APIs and agents. The through-line is that all AI companies are private and no public dataset tracks enterprise AI spend, making Ramp's research an unusual empirical window into a market that otherwise runs on press releases.
key_points: Ramp processes receipts and invoices for 50,000 businesses covering $100 billion of annual spend, and because clients must upload tax-compliant documentation, Ramp sees not just which AI vendor a company pays but which specific model: Opus versus Sonnet, Claude Code subscriptions versus raw API calls, or Gemini Flash.

The Ramp economics team publishes research on AI spend trends specifically because no public dataset tracks how private US businesses adopt and pay for AI tools, filling a gap that affects both technical buyers and finance teams trying to make informed purchasing decisions.

Voice AI in customer service faces a structural bottleneck: LLMs need thinking time (10 to 20 seconds) to reason well, but voice interfaces require near-zero latency, and omni models that process voice and generate intelligence simultaneously represent a different model family with its own capability ceiling.

Ramp tracks AI spend at multiple resolutions: total spend by provider, per-model breakdowns, shifts toward higher-intensity use cases such as agents and APIs, and differences in behavior between early adopters and newer enterprise entrants.
